title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot questions Bessie Burch regarding the personal belongings and correspondence of the deceased Mrs. McKinty. Bessie explains that Mrs. McKinty had no close living relatives, though she might receive government mail which she would typically ask a relative named Joe to help manage. Bessie reveals that she packed Mrs. McKinty's personal items, including clothing and small belongings, into a suitcase stored on the top floor of the house.

Poirot requests to examine the suitcase. While Bessie is in the kitchen, Poirot inspects the contents, which include clothing, a silver-backed mirror, a wedding photograph, a ceramic dog, a Bible, and various newspaper clippings concerning flying saucers and prophecies. He discovers two pairs of shoes wrapped in newspapers. One of these is the *Sunday Comet*, dated November 19th, the Sunday before Mrs. McKinty was murdered on November 22nd. Poirot notices that a large rectangular section has been cut out of the center of this newspaper. Recalling that Mrs. McKinty had purchased a bottle of ink on the Monday following the paper's publication, Poirot suspects she may have been using the ink to respond to something found in the article.

Poirot examines the *Sunday Comet* article titled "Where are the women of past tragedies now?", which features stories of women involved in historical scandals. He reads about Eva Kane, a maid involved in the famous Craig case. In that instance, Alfred Craig killed his wife via plant alkaloid poisoning, dismembered her, and buried her in his cellar. Eva Kane, who was pregnant at the time, reportedly fled to the New World to start a new life under a different name.
